 (eg	  femoral	  head)	  	   • low	  friction	  plastic	  (eg	  acetabulum)	   	   Friction, Lubrication, Joints Page 4 of 5 slightly permeable and deformable. Under instantaneous force it acts as an elastic material and is able to cushion the effects of sudden forces. However, when pressure is applied for a long time it will deform with loss of fluid. The collagen fibres are aligned in arcades with the fibres perpendicular to the bone at the bone surface, but parallel to it at the surface of the cartilage (see diagram). The perpendicular arrangement at depth helps to anchor the cartilage to the bone. The surface of cartilage with its parallel arrangement of fibres, should be smooth to within 2 to 5 µm. This reduces the friction at the surface which is further reduced by the synovial fluid which acts as a lubricant. Synovial fluid is a dialysate of blood plasma which contains large protein molecules such as hyaluronic acid with a molecular weight of 10 6. This produces a very high viscosity. For low pressures and fast motion of the joint (for example when swinging your leg through during walking) the synovial fluid acts as a boundary lubricant with both cartilage surfaces moving freely over each other due to the lubricating effects of the synovial fluid. As pressure is applied to the joint, the synovial fluid is squeezed from between the two surfaces. Provided the pressure is applied for a limited time (for example, during the time between heel strike and toe off) there will not be time for the fluid to be totally excluded. In fact it is likely that the pressure applied squeezes synovial fluid into the region ahead of the region of greatest pressure thereby allowing its lubrication as the surfaces pass over one another. However, during long periods of pressure such as standing stationary for a long time, it is essential that not all of the synovial fluid is squeezed from between the surfaces. It is believed that there are two mechanisms that maintain lubrication. The first is that pressure applied to the cartilage has a tendency to squeeze synovial fluid from it and therefore replenish the lubricant. However, it is also believed that the synovial fluid loses lighter molecular weight components preferentially while the higher molecular weight hyaluronic acid remains. This causes the viscosity to increase so that it becomes more difficult to exclude the synovial fluid and there is always some lubricant between the cartilage surfaces. This is shown in the adjacent diagrams. Note, that friction is also important in the design of artificial joints. However, a lubricant is not normally used. These usually involve highly polished metal for one component (for example for a femoral prosthesis) and a low friction plastic (e.g. for the acetabulum). DEGENERATIVE JOINT DISEASE Many joint diseases such as osteoarthritis result in changes to the joint which reduce its effectiveness. Such changes include: (i) Roughening of the cartilage
